3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Lee, C. M., Yang, J., Al-shoaibi, A. A. A., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., & Jackson, D. B. (2023). Associations between sexual orientation and early adolescent screen use: Findings from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. Annals of Epidemiology, 82, 64-69.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We conducted a cross-sectional analysis of data from Year 2 of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study (N=10,339, 2018-2020, ages 10-14 years).

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Sexual orientation (sexual minority yes/maybe/no based on single item)
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD sexual orientation item ('Are you gay or bisexual?'; yes, maybe, no, don't understand, decline to answer)
3C_Exposure.modalities: N/A - sexual orientation is the independent variable; screen time is the outcome
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Categorical (sexual minority yes vs. no; possible sexual minority 'maybe' vs. no)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Participants reported their sexual orientation ('Are you gay or bisexual?'; yes, maybe, no, don't understand the question, decline to answer). Participants who responded 'yes' were considered sexual minority adolescents, 'maybe' were considered possible sexual minority adolescents, and 'no' were considered heterosexual adolescents.
3C_Exposure.notes: NOTE: In this paper, screen time/problematic screen use are the OUTCOMES, not the exposure. Sexual orientation is the independent variable.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Total recreational screen time (hours/day) and modality-specific screen time; problematic screen use scores (VGAQ, SMAQ, MPIQ)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey, Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Recreational screen use for the following modalities was determined using adolescents' self-reported hours of use on a typical weekday and weekend: multi-player gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing the internet, and watching/streaming movies, videos, or TV. Total typical daily screen use was calculated as the weighted sum. Problematic screen use was assessed via VGAQ, SMAQ, and MPIQ.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multiple linear regression with propensity weights; separate models for each screen time modality and problematic use measure
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, study site
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Multiple linear regression analyses were conducted to estimate cross-sectional associations between sexual minority identification and possible sexual minority identification (compared to heterosexual) and recreational screen time (eight modalities in total) as well as three forms of problematic screen use (video game, social media, mobile phone), adjusting for potential confounders including sex, race/ethnicity, parent education, household income, and study site. Propensity weights were applied to yield representative estimates based on the American Community Survey from the U.S. Census.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: 24 separate regression models (12 unadjusted + 12 adjusted) for sexual minority (yes vs no) and 24 for possible sexual minority (maybe vs no).

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours/day difference in total screen time
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: sexual minority (compared to heterosexual) identification was associated with 3.72 (95% CI 2.96-4.47) more hours of daily recreational screen time
0.94 (95% CI 0.63-1.25) more hours of watching videos
0.46 (95% CI 0.25, 0.67) more hours of using social media
sexual minority status was associated with a 0.49 (95% CI 0.33-0.64) higher problematic video game use score
sexual minority status among the subset reporting social media use was associated with a 0.46 (95% CI 0.32-0.60) higher problematic social media use score
among those reporting mobile phone use, a 0.56 (95% CI 0.41-0.70) higher problematic mobile phone use score

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Possible sexual minority identification (maybe vs. no)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Possible sexual minority identification (responding 'maybe') was associated with 1.58 (95% CI 0.92-2.24) more hours of screen time compared to heterosexual identification.

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The effect sizes here are relatively large (3.72 hours/day more total screen time for sexual minority youth), so the lack of small-ES discussion is less concerning.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Parents, teachers, and pediatricians should be aware that sexual minority adolescents are more likely to exhibit problematic screen use than their heterosexual peers and look for warning signs for problematic screen use including mood modification, conflict, tolerance, withdrawal, and relapse.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion frames findings as revealing disparities in screen time and problematic screen use by sexual orientation. It suggests that sexual minority youth may turn to screens for connection and support, noting that YouTube and internet resources serve as sources of identity exploration content. The paper acknowledges that the cross-sectional design prevents causal inference and notes the young age of the sample relative to typical sexual orientation identity development (mean 17.8 years). Recommendations focus on awareness and personalized guidance rather than blanket screen time reduction.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Note that screen time is the OUTCOME in this paper, with sexual orientation as the independent variable. The 3.72-hour difference is large but may partly reflect confounding with other factors.
